symbols: central cross/tree form — the cross of Christ as axis mundi, the cosmic tree connecting heaven and earth
gold background — divine glory, heavenly transcendence, and the Byzantine tradition of sacred space
triptych format — liturgical altarpiece form, framing the crucifixion as an act of worship
multitude of figures and scenes — all of human history and culture gathered beneath the cross
two white circles at the base — possibly sun and moon, or the cosmic witnesses to the crucifixion (Luke 23:44-45)
branching tree-like form — the Tree of Life from Genesis and Revelation, now identified with the cross
figure at the apex — Christ or a divine figure crowning the cosmic tree
description: A large triptych painting set against a golden background. The central composition forms a massive tree-like or brain-like shape that branches outward from a central trunk, filled entirely with densely packed miniature scenes, figures, and imagery drawn from diverse world cultures, mythologies, and historical periods. Hundreds of tiny vignettes are woven together into the organic form. At the top center, a figure appears to crown the composition. At the base, two white circular forms flank the trunk of the tree. The overall shape suggests both a tree of life and a cross.
theological_description: Klarwein's Crucifixion reimagines the cross as the axis mundi — the cosmic center around which all human history, culture, and spiritual aspiration revolves. The tree-like form deliberately conflates the cross of Golgotha with the Tree of Life from Eden (Genesis 2:9) and the eschatological Tree of Life whose leaves are 'for the healing of the nations' (Revelation 22:2). This identification of cross and tree is ancient in Christian theology: the early Church Fathers taught that the wood of the cross reversed the curse of the forbidden tree, so that where death entered through a tree, life was restored through a tree. The triptych format and gold leaf background invoke the Byzantine altarpiece tradition, sacralizing the entire composition as an object of contemplation. The multitude of figures from all cultures and epochs gathered within the tree enacts Paul's cosmic Christology: 'in him all things hold together' (Colossians 1:17), and 'God was pleased to reconcile to himself all things, whether things on earth or things in heaven, by making peace through his blood, shed on the cross' (Colossians 1:20). The two luminous circles at the base may recall the cosmic darkening at the crucifixion (Luke 23:44-45), when the sun itself bore witness to the death of its Creator. This is the Crucifixion as seen from eternity — not a local event on a hill outside Jerusalem, but the still point of the turning world upon which all creation hinges.

scripture_refs.reason: The tree-like form of the cross recalls the Tree of Life in Eden, which Christian typology identifies as prefiguring the cross
The eschatological Tree of Life whose leaves heal the nations finds its fulfillment in the cross depicted here as a cosmic tree
The image of all creation gathered within the cross directly enacts Paul's theology that in Christ all things hold together and are reconciled through His blood
The white circles at the base may reference the cosmic darkening of sun and moon at the crucifixion
Jesus declared 'when I am lifted up from the earth, I will draw all people to myself' — the cross as universal center drawing all humanity
God's plan to unite all things in heaven and on earth under Christ is visually enacted in this cosmic crucifixion
Paul's declaration that through the cross the world has been crucified to him — the cross as the lens through which all reality is reinterpreted
